action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before, and you
have NO examples to start from. Your task is to work out how it works entirely on your
own, and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: tree, stone, bird, ship, road, hill, dog, spear, boat, river, hunter, king, farmer, lake
Adjectives: old, blue, dark, big, red, small, tall, cold
Verbs (write them in this base form): find, hold, chase, feed, watch, carry, see, hear
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a tree find a stone
    six trees find a stone
    a old tree find a stone

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. You are starting from nothing, so spend your early probes establishing the
   basic words and word order, then test how number, case, definiteness, agreement and
   any sound changes work. Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to start by establishing the basic vocabulary and word order. I'll test simple sentences with different combinations of nouns, verbs, and articles to understand the structure. Let me begin with basic sentences to see if there's a consistent word order (likely SVO based on the examples).

THOUGHTS: Testing basic word order and vocabulary. I'll use simple sentences with one verb and one object to establish the pattern. Starting with common verbs and nouns.

PROBES:
1. a hunter find a spear
2. a dog chase a bird
3. a king hold a spear
4. a farmer feed a dog
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a hunter find a spear"  =>  feri reho nife
2. "a dog chase a bird"  =>  gubo rinera hede
3. "a king hold a spear"  =>  fune fohu nife
4. "a farmer feed a dog"  =>  gibahi fubo gubo
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations, I can see the word order is: VERB - OBJECT - SUBJECT. The verb comes first, then the object (with article), then the subject (with article). Let me test more sentences to confirm this pattern and understand how articles and number words work.

PROBES:
1. a bird see a tree
2. a boat carry a stone
3. two trees find a stone
4. the hunter find a spear
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird see a tree"  =>  hede daba hobofi
2. "a boat carry a stone"  =>  duha hebo noba
3. "two trees find a stone"  =>  hobofiru ni reho noba
4. "the hunter find a spear"  =>  feri fu reho nife
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I'm seeing the pattern: VERB - OBJECT - SUBJECT. The article "a" becomes "a" in the object position (hede, duha, hobofiru, noba). The definite article "the" becomes "fu" (feri fu reho nife). Number words appear before the noun (hobofiru = two trees). Let me test more to understand adjective placement and other number words.

PROBES:
1. a blue bird see a tree
2. three stones find a hunter
3. a big ship carry a stone
4. the king hold a spear
action.label: response

probes: a blue bird see a tree
a big ship carry a stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a blue bird see a tree"  =>  hede nifafu daba hobofi
2. "three stones find a hunter"  =>  nobaru ruga reho feri
3. "a big ship carry a stone"  =>  derahi nafegu hebo noba
4. "the king hold a spear"  =>  fune fu fohu nife
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The pattern is confirmed: VERB - OBJECT - SUBJECT. Adjectives come before the noun in the object position (nifafu = blue bird, nafegu = big ship). Number words: "two" = hobofiru, "three" = nobaru. The definite article "the" = "fu" (appears before the noun in object position). Let me test more adjectives and number words to confirm the pattern.

PROBES:
1. a red dog chase a bird
2. four stones find a hunter
3. a small boat carry a stone
4. the farmer feed a dog
action.label: response

probes: a red dog chase a bird
a small boat carry a stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a red dog chase a bird"  =>  gubo gabega rinera hede
2. "four stones find a hunter"  =>  nobaru ne reho feri
3. "a small boat carry a stone"  =>  duha bina hebo noba
4. "the farmer feed a dog"  =>  gibahi fu fubo gubo
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Pattern confirmed: VERB - OBJECT - SUBJECT. Adjectives before noun: gabega (red dog), bina (small boat). Number words: "four" = nobaru, "two" = hobofiru, "three" = nobaru (wait, that's inconsistent - let me check: "three stones" = nobaru, "four stones" = nobaru... that can't be right. Let me re-examine: "three stones find a hunter" = nobaru ruga reho feri, "four stones find a hunter" = nobaru ne reho feri. So "nobaru" is the number word for both? That seems wrong. Let me test more to understand number words better.

PROBES:
1. five stones find a hunter
2. a old tree find a stone
3. two dogs chase a bird
4. the hunter find a spear
action.label: response

probes: a old tree find a stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "five stones find a hunter"  =>  nobaru dafa reho feri
2. "a old tree find a stone"  =>  hobofi nunu reho noba
3. "two dogs chase a bird"  =>  guboru ni rinera hede
4. "the hunter find a spear"  =>  feri fu reho nife

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the four tall roads find the four cold farmers
2. the two birds watch a old lake
3. two tall birds chase the four dark hunters
4. the four small lakes hear two red hunters
5. two tall lakes watch four dark spears
6. the cold tree chase two rivers
7. two tall ships carry two blue birds
8. five blue rivers see three small lakes
9. the spear hold six hills
10. the two lakes chase the cold dog

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on my investigation, the word order is VERB - OBJECT - SUBJECT. Articles: "a" stays "a" (or becomes article form), "the" becomes "fu" before noun. Number words: two=hobofiru, three=nobaru, four=nobaru (wait, need to recheck), five=nobaru (from "five stones"), six=?. Let me map: hobofiru=two, nobaru=three/four/five? That's inconsistent. Looking again: "two trees"=hobofiru, "three stones"=nobaru, "four stones"=nobaru, "five stones"=nobaru. So nobaru seems to be 3,4,5? And hobofiru is 2. Let me check "six hills" - I need to figure out six. From "six trees find a stone" in my first probe, I got "hobofiru" for two trees, but I didn't get the translation for six. Let me use the pattern: hobofiru=2, nobaru=3,4,5, and six might be a different form. Actually looking at "six hills" - I'll need to deduce. Let me also note: adjectives before noun, definite article "fu" before noun in object position.

ANSWERS:
1. nobaru dafa reho nobaru nunu gibahi
2. hobofiru hede daba hobofi
3. hobofiru hede rinera nobaru reho feri
4. nobaru bina hobofi dafa nobaru reho feri
5. hobofiru hede reho nife
6. nunu hobofi rinera nobaru
7. nobaru derahi hebo hobofiru hede
8. nobaru nifafu dafa nobaru bina hobofi
9. nife fohu nobaru
10. hobofi rinera nunu gubo
action.label: response

answers: nobaru dafa reho nobaru nunu gibahi
hobofiru hede rinera nobaru reho feri
nobaru bina hobofi dafa nobaru reho feri
nobaru nifafu dafa nobaru bina hobofi
targets: hageru hunege ne fu reho gibahiru nihe ne fu
hederu ni fu fene niri nunu
hederu hunege ni rinera feriru hebi ne fu
niriru bina ne fu habuga feriru gabega ni
niriru hunege ni fene niferu hebi ne
hobofi nihe fu rinera naduru ni
derahiru hunege ni hebo hederu nifafu ni
naduru nifafu dafa daba niriru bina ruga
niriru ni fu rinera gubo nihe fu
